abstract: Scholarly analysis of Jesus’ mission in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) has historically overlooked the significance of its geographical setting. This lack of scrutiny is closely tied to the identity of the "Jew" involved in a dispute with John the Baptist’s disciples in John 3:25. By examining the spatial framework of Jesus' ministry across John 2:13–4:54, this study offers fresh insights into these issues. The Fourth Gospel appears to organize Jesus’ mission according to a geographical progression strikingly similar to the mandate in Acts 1:8, where witnesses travel from Jerusalem through Judea and Samaria to the ends of the earth. In John’s account, this trajectory ultimately leads Jesus to Galilee, ensuring that all of God’s people have the opportunity to hear his message and come to faith prior to the escalating conflicts with religious leaders detailed in chapters 5–10. Within this context of a comprehensive mission to Israel, the term Ἰουδαῖος in John 3:25 is better understood as a "Judean" rather than a "Jew" in the broader sense.
